findings.description: API key auth is silently SKIPPED when SOVEREIGNFORGE_API_KEY env var is empty. Line 72: 'if API_KEY and x_api_key != API_KEY' — if API_KEY is empty string (the default), the guard short-circuits and EVERY POST endpoint is unprotected. An attacker on localhost (or any LAN peer if binding changes) can start/stop the pipeline, toggle trading pairs, and modify config without any credential.
findings.recommendation: Fail closed: if API_KEY is empty, REJECT all mutation requests. Add 'if not API_KEY: raise HTTPException(503, "API key not configured")' at the top of verify_api_key.

findings.description: config/api_keys.json is NOT listed in .gitignore. It is tracked by git. Currently contains empty placeholder values, but the moment a developer fills in real exchange API keys they will be committed to version history. Extracting secrets from git history is trivial (git log -p, BFG Repo-Cleaner).
findings.recommendation: Add 'config/api_keys.json' and 'config/secrets*' to .gitignore immediately. Use a .json.example file for the template. Rotate any keys that have ever been committed.

findings.description: execute_query() and execute_update() accept raw SQL strings and pass them directly to the database. Any caller that builds queries via string concatenation or f-strings can inject arbitrary SQL. Even with asyncpg's parameterized interface, the 'query' parameter itself is an unvalidated string. There is no query whitelist, no prepared statement enforcement, and no input sanitization layer.
findings.recommendation: Remove execute_query() and execute_update() raw SQL methods. Replace with purpose-built methods that use parameterized queries exclusively. If raw SQL is required, add an allowlist of permitted query patterns.

findings.description: WebSocket endpoint at /ws accepts unlimited-size messages with no authentication. Line 756: 'data = await websocket.receive_text()'. An attacker can: (1) open 10 connections to exhaust MAX_WS_CONNECTIONS, denying service to legitimate dashboard clients, (2) send arbitrarily large JSON payloads to exhaust server memory, (3) send messages at high frequency with no rate limiting. There is no origin validation on WebSocket upgrade requests.
findings.recommendation: Add message size limits (e.g. 4KB max). Add origin validation on WebSocket handshake. Add per-connection rate limiting. Require auth token in WebSocket connection query params.

findings.description: Multiple endpoints (lines 942, 987, 1034, 1078, 1096) catch exceptions and return str(e) directly to the client. Exception messages from Python can contain file paths, module names, database connection strings, and stack trace fragments. Attack scenario: trigger an error in /api/exchanges/status to learn internal path structure and library versions.
findings.recommendation: Never return raw exception messages. Log the full exception server-side, return a generic error message to the client. Example: return {'error': 'Internal server error', 'code': 'EXCHANGE_STATUS_ERROR'}.

findings.description: No rate limiting on any endpoint. The comment on line 78 says 'Rate limiting: deferred'. This means an attacker can: (1) brute-force the API key via rapid POST requests to any mutation endpoint, (2) DoS the server by flooding GET endpoints that perform file I/O (training log parsing, JSON file reads), (3) exhaust file descriptors via rapid WebSocket connections.
findings.recommendation: Add rate limiting immediately. Use slowapi or a custom middleware. Critical limits: auth endpoints 5 req/min, mutation endpoints 10 req/min, read endpoints 60 req/min.

findings.description: CORS allows credentials (allow_credentials=True) with specific origins, but there is zero CSRF protection on state-changing POST endpoints. If a user has the dashboard open in a browser and visits a malicious site, the attacker can forge POST requests to /api/paper-trading/start, /api/pipeline/start, /api/config/toggle-pair etc. The browser will include cookies/credentials automatically.
findings.recommendation: Add CSRF token validation. Use a double-submit cookie pattern or require a custom header (e.g. X-Requested-With) that cannot be set by cross-origin form submissions.

findings.description: The 172.x.x.x check on line 164 treats ALL 172.* addresses as private. RFC 1918 only reserves 172.16.0.0-172.31.255.255. Addresses like 172.1.2.3 or 172.32.0.0 are PUBLIC routable IPs. An attacker establishing connections via public 172.x IPs would bypass the external connection detector, allowing data exfiltration to appear as 'local' traffic.
findings.recommendation: Check the full RFC 1918 range: remote_ip.startswith('172.') and 16 <= int(remote_ip.split('.')[1]) <= 31. Better: use ipaddress.ip_address(remote_ip).is_private from the Python standard library.

findings.description: All parse_ticker_message() methods across 7 exchange connectors (BinanceWebSocket, CoinbaseWebSocket, KrakenWebSocket, etc.) accept raw JSON from untrusted WebSocket feeds with no size limit validation and no schema enforcement beyond basic key checks. A compromised or malicious exchange feed could send crafted payloads with extreme float values (1e308), deeply nested objects, or oversized strings to cause memory exhaustion or numeric overflow. The MarketData __post_init__ validator catches NaN/Inf but does not check for extreme magnitudes.
findings.recommendation: Add message size limits before JSON parsing (reject messages > 64KB). Validate numeric ranges (price > 0, price < 1e9). Use pydantic or similar for strict schema validation of exchange messages.

findings.description: Telegram bot token is read from TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN environment variable with empty string default. The token grants full control of the bot (read messages, send messages to any chat). Environment variables are visible via /proc/PID/environ on Linux, in process listings, and in container orchestrator dashboards. No scoping or rotation mechanism exists.
findings.recommendation: Store the token in an encrypted secrets file or a secrets manager. At minimum, validate the token format before use. Add token rotation support.

findings.description: paper_trading_start() and pipeline_start() launch subprocesses with environment variables derived from os.environ. While the script paths are hardcoded (not user-controlled), the entire parent environment is passed to child processes via env={**os.environ, ...}. Any malicious env vars (LD_PRELOAD, PYTHONPATH manipulation) in the parent environment propagate to child processes.
findings.recommendation: Sanitize the environment passed to subprocesses. Create a minimal env dict with only required variables instead of inheriting all of os.environ.

findings.description: DATABASE_URL from environment variable may contain username, password, host, and port in the connection string (e.g. postgresql://user:password@host:5432/db). This string is used directly in both asyncpg.create_pool() and SQLAlchemy create_engine(). If an error occurs, the connection string may be logged or leaked in exception messages.
findings.recommendation: Parse DATABASE_URL and redact credentials before any logging. Use separate env vars for host/user/password/database. Ensure error handlers never log the raw connection string.

findings.description: Exchange connections via ccxt use default SSL settings without explicit certificate verification configuration. While ccxt enables SSL by default, there is no explicit ssl_context configuration, no certificate pinning, and no check that HTTPS is enforced. A MITM attacker on the network could potentially intercept exchange API calls if system CA certificates are compromised.
findings.recommendation: Explicitly configure ssl_context with certificate verification. Consider certificate pinning for critical exchange endpoints. Add 'verify_ssl': True to exchange config.

findings.description: Pipeline reads API keys from config/api_keys.json using a hardcoded relative path. The file is loaded into memory as a plain dict. No encryption at rest, no access logging, no file permission checks. Combined with the .gitignore gap, keys are at high risk of exposure.
findings.recommendation: Encrypt api_keys.json at rest using Fernet or age. Check file permissions (0600) before reading. Log all access to the keys file. Use a secrets manager for production.

findings.description: WebSocket validator hardcodes USDT pairs (btcusdt, ethusdt, xrpusdt, etc.) on line 84. This violates the MiCA compliance rule stated in the project: 'NEVER use USDT. Only USDC and RLUSD stablecoins.' While this is a test/validation module, it could lead to accidental USDT trading pair configuration.
findings.recommendation: Replace all USDT pairs with USDC equivalents: btcusdc, ethusdc, etc. Add a compliance check that rejects any pair containing 'USDT'.

findings.description: get_agent_report() accepts user-supplied agent_name and constructs a file path: reports_dir / f'{name}.json'. While the name is somewhat constrained by URL routing, there is no explicit sanitization against path traversal characters (../, null bytes). An attacker could potentially read arbitrary JSON files by passing names like '../../../etc/passwd'.
findings.recommendation: Sanitize agent_name: reject any name containing '/', '\', '..', or null bytes. Use Path.resolve() and verify the result is still within reports_dir.

findings.description: verify_local_execution() connects to 8.8.8.8:80 (Google DNS) as a fallback to determine the local IP. This creates an actual outbound network connection during a 'local execution' check, which is ironic. If DNS is being monitored, this connection reveals the system is running SovereignForge.
findings.recommendation: Use socket.gethostname() or platform-specific methods to determine local IP without making outbound connections.

findings.description: get_price_history() falls back to synthetic data (line 438-440) when exchange data is unavailable. If this synthetic data is used for trading decisions without clear flagging, it could lead to trades based on fabricated prices. The synthetic data generation is deterministic and predictable.
findings.recommendation: Raise an exception instead of returning synthetic data. If fallback is required, clearly mark the data as synthetic and prevent it from triggering trade execution.

findings.description: Server binds to 127.0.0.1:8420 — good. However, the port is configurable via SOVEREIGNFORGE_DASHBOARD_PORT env var and the host is not configurable. A reverse proxy or Docker network configuration could still expose this.
findings.recommendation: Document that 127.0.0.1 binding is intentional and must not be changed. Add a startup warning if running behind a reverse proxy without TLS.

findings.description: User-Agent header set to 'SovereignForge/1.0' reveals the application name and version to all exchange WebSocket endpoints. This enables fingerprinting of the trading system.
findings.recommendation: Use a generic User-Agent or match common browser/library user agents to avoid system fingerprinting.

summary: SovereignForge has a health score of 62/100. Three CRITICAL findings demand immediate attention: (1) API key authentication silently disables itself when the env var is unset, leaving all mutation endpoints open; (2) config/api_keys.json is tracked by git, creating a credentials leak vector; (3) raw SQL execution methods in database.py enable SQL injection from any caller. Six HIGH findings include missing rate limiting, absent CSRF protection, WebSocket input validation gaps, error messages leaking internals, an RFC 1918 range check bug in network monitoring, and unauthenticated WebSocket endpoint. The codebase shows good security awareness (security headers middleware, config secret redaction, paper trading safety gates, localhost binding) but has significant gaps in defense-in-depth that must be resolved before any live trading.
recommendations: IMMEDIATE: Fix verify_api_key to fail closed when SOVEREIGNFORGE_API_KEY is unset. Add config/api_keys.json to .gitignore and purge from git history.
SHORT-TERM: Remove raw SQL methods from database.py. Add rate limiting (slowapi). Add WebSocket message size limits and authentication. Fix RFC 1918 range check in personal_security.py.

MEDIUM-TERM: Add CSRF protection. Sanitize subprocess environments. Replace USDT pairs in websocket_validator.py with USDC. Add path traversal protection to agent report endpoint. Encrypt secrets at rest.
